timelines.title: Kīlauea — a history of fire
timelines.intro: One of the most active volcanoes on Earth and, in Hawaiian tradition, the home of the goddess Pele. Continuously monitored since 1912.

events.title: Deadly explosive eruption
events.text: A violent steam-driven explosion killed part of the army of chief Keōua crossing the Kaʻū Desert. Their footprints, pressed into wet ash, are still preserved — the deadliest known volcanic eruption in what is now the United States.

events.title: First written eyewitness account
events.text: Missionary William Ellis and a party became the first non-Hawaiians to describe the roiling lava lake of Halemaʻumaʻu, launching two centuries of scientific fascination.

events.title: Hawaiian Volcano Observatory founded
events.text: Geologist Thomas Jaggar established HVO on the caldera rim, pioneering continuous volcano monitoring. It is the oldest of the USGS volcano observatories.

events.title: Halemaʻumaʻu explosions
events.text: As the lava lake drained, groundwater flashed to steam and blasted multi-ton blocks around the crater over two weeks, killing one photographer and doubling the crater's width.

events.title: Kīlauea Iki lava fountains
events.text: Fountains from Kīlauea Iki reached a record 1,900 ft (580 m), building the Puʻu Puaʻi cinder cone and a lava lake whose crust hikers still cross today.

events.title: The Puʻu ʻŌʻō eruption
events.text: A 35-year East Rift Zone eruption — one of the longest in recorded history. Its lava buried the town of Kalapana in 1990 and repeatedly reshaped the coastline before the vent collapsed in 2018.

events.title: Lower Puna eruption & summit collapse
events.text: Fissure 8 in Leilani Estates fed a river of lava that destroyed ~700 homes and filled Kapoho Bay, while the summit lava lake drained and Halemaʻumaʻu collapsed dramatically over three months.

events.title: Lava returns to the summit
events.text: A new eruption refilled Halemaʻumaʻu with a lava lake, drawing crowds to the overlooks in Hawaiʻi Volcanoes National Park.

events.title: Episodic summit fountaining
events.text: Repeated short summit eruptions and lava-fountaining episodes continued, tracked live by HVO — see the current alert level and webcams on this map.

timelines.title: Mauna Loa — the largest active volcano on Earth
timelines.intro: By volume the biggest volcano on the planet, covering half of Hawaiʻi Island. It has erupted 33 times since 1843.

events.title: First documented eruption
events.text: The first well-recorded historical eruption of Mauna Loa opened the modern observational record of the mountain.

events.title: Bombing the lava flow
events.text: As lava threatened Hilo's water supply, Thomas Jaggar asked the Army Air Corps to bomb the flow's channels to divert it — an audacious, much-debated experiment led by then-Lt. Col. George S. Patton's era airmen.

events.title: The secret wartime eruption
events.text: An eruption during WWII was hushed under blackout rules so its glow couldn't guide enemy ships toward the islands.

events.title: Southwest Rift Zone races to the sea
events.text: One of the most voluminous flank eruptions on record reached the ocean in about three hours, cutting the belt highway and destroying the Kona village of Hoʻokena-mauka.

events.title: Lava toward Hilo
events.text: A Northeast Rift Zone eruption sent flows to within about 4 miles of Hilo before stopping — the last eruption for 38 years.

events.title: Reawakening
events.text: After 38 quiet years Mauna Loa erupted again from its Northeast Rift Zone, sending lava across the flank toward (but not over) Saddle Road, watched worldwide.

timelines.title: Hilo & the great tsunamis
timelines.intro: Hilo's crescent bay funnels and amplifies waves, making it the most tsunami-struck city in the United States. Two disasters reshaped it forever.

events.title: April Fools' Day tsunami
events.text: A magnitude-8.6 Aleutian earthquake sent waves that killed 159 people in Hawaiʻi — 96 in Hilo alone — with no warning. At Laupāhoehoe, 24 students and teachers at a seaside school were swept away.

events.title: A warning system is born
events.text: The disaster led directly to the creation of what became the Pacific Tsunami Warning Center, headquartered in Hawaiʻi.

events.title: The Chile tsunami
events.text: The largest earthquake ever recorded (M9.5, Chile) sent a tsunami across the Pacific that killed 61 people in Hilo and flattened the Waiākea district — despite warnings many did not heed.

events.title: Rebuilding as buffer parks
events.text: Rather than rebuild the lethal low-lying bayfront, planners converted Waiākea into green space — today's Liliʻuokalani Gardens and bayfront parks are that tsunami buffer.

timelines.title: Kamehameha the Great & Puʻukoholā
timelines.intro: The war temple above Kawaihae is bound to the rise of the chief who first united the Hawaiian Islands.

events.title: Birth in Kohala
events.text: Kamehameha was born in North Kohala, tradition tying his birth to the appearance of a bright comet (likely Halley's) that a prophecy said heralded a slayer of chiefs.

events.title: Building the war temple
events.text: Told by a prophet he would conquer all the islands if he built a great heiau to his war god at Puʻukoholā, Kamehameha's people moved stones hand-to-hand from miles away.

events.title: The dedication
events.text: His chief rival Keōua came to the dedication and was killed and offered on the altar — removing the last challenge to Kamehameha's control of Hawaiʻi Island.

events.title: Conquest of the islands
events.text: With guns, ships and Western advisers, Kamehameha invaded Maui, Molokaʻi and Oʻahu, winning the decisive Battle of Nuʻuanu where warriors were driven over the Pali cliffs.

events.title: One kingdom
events.text: Kauaʻi's chief submitted by negotiation, completing the unification of all the Hawaiian Islands under a single ruler for the first time.

events.title: Death at Kamakahonu
events.text: Kamehameha died at his Kailua-Kona compound; within months the kapu system he upheld was overthrown, on the eve of the missionaries' arrival.

timelines.title: The Kohala Ditch
timelines.intro: A 1900s feat of hand engineering that made sugar possible in dry Kohala — and nearly killed the men who built it.

events.title: An impossible contract
events.text: Sugar baron John Hind hired engineer Michael O'Shaughnessy (later of San Francisco's Hetch Hetchy) to carry water from the wet Kohala mountains to the arid cane fields.

events.title: Built by hand in 18 months
events.text: Around 600 laborers — mostly Japanese immigrants — dug 22.5 miles of tunnels and wooden flumes through sheer gulches. Roughly 17 workers died in the dangerous work.

events.title: Water arrives
events.text: The completed ditch delivered tens of millions of gallons a day, and Kohala's plantations boomed for the next seven decades.

events.title: The sugar era ends
events.text: Kohala Sugar Company closed, and the ditch's purpose faded — though it kept watering ranches and small farms.

events.title: Earthquake damage
events.text: The M6.7 Kīholo Bay earthquake collapsed tunnels and ended the popular 'Flumin' da Ditch' kayak tours for years; partial restoration followed.

timelines.title: Kalapana — a town beneath the lava
timelines.intro: A beloved Puna village and its famous black-sand beach, lost to Kīlauea in a single dramatic year.

events.title: Kaimū black-sand beach
events.text: Kalapana's Kaimū Beach, fringed with coconut palms, was among the most photographed places in Hawaiʻi and a cultural heart of Puna.

events.title: The flows arrive
events.text: Lava from the Puʻu ʻŌʻō eruption advanced through Kalapana over months, burning more than 100 homes, the store, and burying Kaimū Beach under dozens of feet of rock.

events.title: Saving the Painted Church
events.text: The historic 1928 Star of the Sea 'Painted Church' was lifted onto a truck and moved out of the lava's path; it survives today as a roadside landmark.

events.title: A new beach is born
events.text: A fresh black-sand beach and a young planted coconut grove have formed on top of the 1990 flow — the island rebuilding itself.

timelines.title: Kapoho — twice taken by Kīlauea
timelines.intro: A lush lower-Puna community famous for its tide pools, destroyed by eruptions 58 years apart.

events.title: The village is buried
events.text: A lower East Rift Zone eruption destroyed Kapoho village and its farms; the lava stopped just short of the Cape Kumukahi lighthouse, sparing the tower.

events.title: Tide pools & Vacationland
events.text: Over decades the Kapoho tide pools and the Vacationland/Kapoho Beach Lots became a snorkeling paradise built around warm, spring-fed pools.

events.title: Fissure 8 fills the bay
events.text: The 2018 eruption's massive channelized flow buried Vacationland, Kapoho Beach Lots and the entire Kapoho Bay and tide-pool system, adding new land to the coastline.

timelines.title: Kealakekua Bay & Captain Cook
timelines.intro: A sacred bay that became the site of one of the most famous encounters — and deaths — in Pacific history.

events.title: Arrival during Makahiki
events.text: Captain James Cook sailed into Kealakekua Bay during the Makahiki festival honoring Lono; he was received with extraordinary ceremony at Hikiau Heiau, possibly as an embodiment of the god.

events.title: The death of Cook
events.text: After leaving and returning to repair a mast, a dispute over a stolen boat escalated; Cook was killed in the shallows at Kaʻawaloa on 14 February 1779.

events.title: The monument
events.text: A white obelisk was erected at Kaʻawaloa to mark the spot; the small parcel of land is deeded to the United Kingdom.

events.title: Marine reserve
events.text: The bay is a protected Marine Life Conservation District — some of the island's best-preserved coral reef and a resident pod of spinner dolphins.

timelines.title: Parker Ranch & the paniolo
timelines.intro: From wild cattle to one of America's largest ranches — the cradle of Hawaiian cowboy culture in Waimea.

events.title: Cattle arrive
events.text: Captain George Vancouver gave longhorn cattle to Kamehameha, who placed a kapu protecting them; within decades wild herds overran the uplands.

events.title: John Palmer Parker
events.text: A young New England sailor jumped ship, befriended Kamehameha, and was later given permission to hunt and manage the wild cattle — the ranch's beginning.

events.title: The paniolo
events.text: Mexican vaqueros were brought in to teach cattle handling; Hawaiians adapted their skills, dress and music into the distinct paniolo (cowboy) culture — decades before the American Wild West.

events.title: The ranch is founded
events.text: Parker formally established the ranch that would grow to cover much of North Hawaiʻi's grasslands.

events.title: Left to the community
events.text: Last Parker heir Richard Smart died and willed the ranch to a charitable trust that still funds Waimea's schools, hospital and community.

timelines.title: Mauna Kea — sacred summit, window to the universe
timelines.intro: The highest point in the Pacific: a wahi pana (storied place) at the piko of the island, and home to the world's premier astronomy.

events.title: Sacred realm
events.text: In Hawaiian tradition the summit region is the meeting place of Earth Mother and Sky Father and home to deities; Lake Waiau near the top is a sacred site, and the mountain holds the largest adze quarry in the Pacific.

events.title: First summit road
events.text: A road to the summit opened the peak to research, prized for its dry, dark, stable air above 40% of the atmosphere.

events.title: UH 2.2-metre telescope
events.text: The University of Hawaiʻi's telescope proved the site's world-class quality and launched Mauna Kea's astronomy era.

events.title: CFHT, UKIRT & IRTF
events.text: A wave of international telescopes — Canada-France-Hawaii, the UK Infrared Telescope, and NASA's IRTF (built for planetary missions) — arrived within a year.

events.title: The Keck telescopes
events.text: The twin 10-metre Kecks, using revolutionary segmented mirrors, became the largest optical/infrared telescopes on Earth.

events.title: Subaru & Gemini North
events.text: Japan's 8.2-metre Subaru and the 8.1-metre Gemini North added giant single-mirror telescopes to the summit.

events.title: The TMT conflict
events.text: Plans for the Thirty Meter Telescope sparked sustained protests defending the mountain as sacred land, forcing a broad reckoning over stewardship of the summit.

timelines.title: Electric vehicles in Hawaiʻi
timelines.intro: Hawaiʻi has among the highest EV adoption rates in the US — a natural fit for an island with short drives, high gas prices, and abundant sun.

events.title: First public chargers
events.text: Hawaiʻi's earliest public charging stations appeared as the first modern highway-capable EVs (Nissan Leaf, Chevy Volt) reached the islands.

events.title: State charging mandate
events.text: A Hawaiʻi law required parking lots with 100+ stalls to provide EV charging — an early nation-leading policy push.

events.title: Hawaiian Electric fast chargers
events.text: The utility began rolling out DC fast-charging hubs across Oʻahu, Maui and Hawaiʻi Island to tackle range anxiety on longer routes.

events.title: Clean-energy target
events.text: Hawaiʻi is legally committed to 100% renewable electricity by 2045, with transportation electrification a key pillar — every charger on this map plugs into that transition.

timelines.title: Waipiʻo — the Valley of the Kings
timelines.intro: A deep, taro-rich valley that was the political and spiritual heart of ancient Hawaiʻi Island.

events.title: Seat of the chiefs
events.text: Waipiʻo was a royal residence and religious center said to have held thousands of people farming vast terraced loʻi (taro pondfields). Great chiefs Līloa and ʻUmi ruled from here.

events.title: Young Kamehameha
events.text: Tradition places pivotal moments of Kamehameha's early life in Waipiʻo, including receiving the war god Kūkāʻilimoku.

events.title: Tsunami devastation
events.text: The April 1946 tsunami surged far up the valley, destroying homes and farms and prompting most residents to leave the valley floor for good.

events.title: Living taro & fragile access
events.text: A small community still farms taro in the valley; the punishingly steep access road is periodically closed for safety, keeping Waipiʻo remote.
